explanation.title: How Java Passes Arguments and Controls Variable Scope

codeExample.title: Bank Account Method Simulation
codeExample.explanation:
- `balance = deposit(balance, 2000.0)` — reassignment required; Java passes primitives by value so the method works on a copy
- `if (amount <= 0) return balance` — guard clause; returns the balance unchanged when input is invalid
- `return new double[]{min, max}` — creates and returns an anonymous array literal with two values
- `double[] minMax = findMinMax(history)` — the returned array is captured in a local variable, accessed by index
- `double bonus` inside the for loop — block-scoped; destroyed when the loop block ends

exercise.title: Write a Grade Statistics Method
exercise.instructions:
Write a static method getStats(int[] marks) that returns a double[] array containing three values in order: [average, minimum, maximum]. In main, call getStats with int[] marks = {72, 88, 95, 61, 83}. Print the average with one decimal place, and the minimum and maximum as integers using printf.

Expected output:
Average: 79.8
Min: 61
Max: 95
hints: 💡 Replace return new double[]{0, 0, 0}; with return new double[]{avg, min, max}; to return computed values.
💡 Print min: System.out.printf("Min: %d%n", (int) stats[1]); — cast to int because %d requires an integer.
💡 Print max the same way: System.out.printf("Max: %d%n", (int) stats[2]);

questions.question: If a Java method modifies a primitive parameter inside its body, what happens to the original variable?
options: The original variable is also modified
Nothing — the method receives a copy, so the original is unchanged
The original variable is set to zero
questions.explanation: Java passes primitive types by value — the method receives a copy of the argument. Modifying the copy inside the method has no effect on the original variable. To change the original, the method must return the new value and the caller must reassign it: num = doubleIt(num).

questions.question: A variable declared inside a for loop body — where is it accessible?
options: Anywhere in the method that contains the loop
Only within the loop body where it is declared
Only in the first iteration of the loop
questions.explanation: Variables declared inside a block — including a for loop body — are block-scoped. They are created fresh on each iteration and destroyed when the block ends. Trying to access a for-loop variable outside the loop causes a compile error because the variable no longer exists at that point.
